Alright, let's dive right into lesson number one of body language starting with something called wrapping your package. Let me explain what that means. Wrapping your package means how you essentially present yourself to the outside world. What do you wear? Do you wear a suit and tie as a man or a power suit as a woman? Do you dress casually? Are your shoes shine correctly or are they dull and scuffed up? I mean, honestly, how do you groom yourself? How do you look essentially, right? So here's the deal. Most people who teach body language will tell you you've got to wear a suit and tie as a man to be taken seriously because as soon as someone lays eyes on you and say, wow, this is a person of power. They got their shit together, so to speak, because they wear a suit and tie, right? Well, that's all fine and well, but let me ask you a question. What if you're a plumber? What if you show up to someone's door wearing a suit and tie as a plumber? They'll be like, uh-oh, this person's going to charge you to awful law for my plumbing because they got to pay for that nice expensive suit and that beautiful car outside. The point is you would never do that. Likewise, if you're a plumber, would you show up to someone's door wearing some beat-up jeans with dirt on them and hanging down so your butt cracker stick it out and your sleeves rolled up with a pack of cigarettes? Obviously not. The point is this, whether you're a stockbroker, a plumber, electrician, a dentist, a doctor, a lawyer, a butcher, a baker, a candlestick baker, it doesn't matter what you are, there is something called the law of congruency, which governs the way you should essentially wrap your package. Meaning, you want to dress in a way, present yourself in a way that the world would think an expert would dress like or look like. You get it? In other words, think of this rationally here. In fact, I'll show you two pictures right now. Imagine you open up your door because you called for an emergency plumber, right? The door opens up, here's two pictures. Which person do you think is more apt to do a great job at fixing your pipes and charging you the right price? Which of the two? The one on the left here who's wearing a suit and tie or this person right here wearing this crisp cut shirt with their name tag, tool belt, which one would you pick? Really? Okay, you're obviously just messing me here because you know it's not true. You would never want to see this person show up at your door for a plumbing job. Obviously, they're simply not a person who's going to sit there and get their fingernails dirty or they're going to charge you a fortune to then go change into their clothes, their work clothes, so to speak. It's ridiculous. You would never do that. Correct. Of course, you would choose this person. This is what a plumber should look like. But let me ask you another question. Watch these two pictures. Which plumber would you choose? The one on the left here with the crisp shirt, name tag, tool belt, groomed correctly, or this plumber here with the grease marks on his face and dressed this way like a dilapidated piece of crap, basically. Which one would you choose? Really, you choose the one that looks like a grease monkey? Come on. You're just messing with me here. Of course, you'd choose the other one. Correct. Of course, you would choose this person, right? I mean, this is a person that looks like he's going to do a great job, keep your house clean, bill you the appropriate amount, give you great customer service, basically knows their stuff. Bottom line is the second that you lay eyes on someone, you rip them apart, judge how they look, and based on that, you say to yourself, are they sharp as a tack? Are they enthusiastic? What they do? Are they experts in their field? And once that person is judged, it's pretty tough for you to change your mind. It takes many subsequent impressions. And bottom line is, as a salesperson, we don't have the time to play catch-up wall. It's so easy simply to dress the appropriate way to set yourself up for absolute greatness in the workplace. Let me give you another example. Women in the workplace, right? They always say that sex sells, right? Well, guess what? Yeah, maybe an adulterate gabbana ad or a prada ad, but in the workplace, do you really take someone seriously who dresses overtly sensual? Yeah, watch. I'll give you two pictures again. Here's the woman to the left and a woman to the right. Which one would you take more seriously in the workplace? Really? You take the woman to the left more seriously. Okay, well, you need some, it's like action-telpeer, but honestly, you're probably just messing me again because that is not someone that you would take seriously. That looks someone that really belongs more in a strip club or a night club. That is not someone that's going to be taken very seriously in the work environment. Correct. Of course, you would choose this person. They look exactly the way you would expect a power person to look. Man, woman, they look sharp on the ball, experts in their field, and because of that, it essentially sets things up for you to defer to control, to give them control of the sale, and hence, they can then start asking you questions and moving you down the straight line. Bottom line is, I don't care what you do for a living, it's very simple. You have to just ask yourself one important question. How would someone expect an expert in my profession to dress? That's the law of congruency, and dress like that. Guys, this is a gimme, it's a throwaway. Anyone could essentially adopt the right dress code, wrap their package correctly, and because of that, set yourself up to have a very easy time of it when you then open up your mouth to speak. Remember, when it comes to body language and also tonality, it's unconscious communication. Those first few seconds, you'll hit that correct pattern so you'll judge the right way. Then after that, based on the words that you say, based on the tonalities that you continue to apply and how you move your body going forward, either they're going to say, yep, I was correct to judge the person that way, or I was incorrect, they'll try to then take back control of the conversation. By wrapping your package the right way, you set yourself up to have a very easy time of it. Now again, certain obvious things here. For a man, insurance, someone in stocks, someone in the banking industry, someone just really a formal individual, a suit and tie is always best. As soon as you see someone with a suit and tie, you say, you know what, that person's got their stuff together. However, that being said, would you want to see me dressed in a shark skin suit with peak lapels and thick chalk pinstripes? You know what you say? You say, oh, I didn't know Jordan's in the mob. It's not that any suit works. You want to have a very neutral suit, black, blue, thin pinstripes. This stuff is very obvious here, but I see this happening all over where people shoot themselves in the foot by simply dressing in appropriately. In other words, I walk into a store because I want to buy a Harley-Davidson and I see the person dressed in a suit and tie. Is that what I want to see? No. I want to see someone in a leather vest, in jeans, in a cool looking belt, still clean cut and groomed correctly. But that's what I expect to see an expert to who rides motorcycles. So I want you to take a very close look at your industry right now. And again, if this boggles your mind a bit here, you're just not naturally goodness. Ask people. Say, what would you expect to see in my industry? It's not that difficult, but wrapping your package is the first linchpin to having great body language. Whether you're a man or a woman, you want to dress for success, but that doesn't mean a suit and tie necessarily. It means dressing in a way that experts in your field are known to dress. All right? And with that, let's take a quick break here, go back into the resource center. We have a quiz and I have a bunch of exercises with pictures to really nail this down. We'll do some interactivity there as well. Okay, so I'll kind of gear it directly to your industry. And then after you're done with that, I'll meet you in the next lesson.
